California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
April 14, 1989 
Honorable Frederick A. Jones 
Judge of the Superior Court 
800 South Victoria Avenue 
Ventura, CA 93009 
Dear Judge Jones: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-169 
This will confirm our telephone conversation of February 
22, 1989, in which we discussed the use of your accumulated 
campaign funds in light of the decision in California Common 
Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles County Superior Court Case No. 
C709383.) 
Since those funds were acquired prior to January 
1, 1989, they would be restricted funds to be used for any 
lawful purpose other than to support or oppose a candidate. 
(Government Code Section 85306.) 
Thus, even though these 
funds were composed of contributions within statutory limits 
and deposited in a campaign bank account for a future 
campaign, the funds could not be used for future campaign 
expenditures unless the California Common Cause decision were 
reversed on appeal. 
I sent you a copy of Section 18536.2 of 
the California Code of Regulations which sets forth 
appropriate uses of restricted funds. 
As I explained to you, the California Common Cause case 
is still subject to appeal. 
We have been advising that no 
action be taken with respect to the disposition of restricted 
funds pending the appeal. 
If the funds now are placed in a 
restricted account, that election probably could not be 
reversed in the event that the California Common Cause 
decision were reversed on appeal. 
Notwithstanding this advice, you have decided not to 
await the outcome of the California Common Cause decision. 
You would prefer to close your campaign bank account and use 
the accumulated funds to repay campaign loans owed by your 
1978 Municipal Court election committee. 
You have outlined 
the steps that you believe would be appropriate to accomplish 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Boy R07 •

this result in your letter of March 14, 1989 (copy enclosed.) 
We have reviewed the proposed sequence and agree that it 
would be an acceptable way to proceed. 
If you have any questions about this letter, I may be 
reached at (916) 322-5901.

Staff Attorney 
CHAMBERS OF 
'lrur ~uprrior (tCourt 
FREDERICK A. JONES 
HALL OF JUSTICE 
800 SOUTH VICTORIA AVENUE 
VENTURA, CA 93009 
(805) 654-2915 
March 14, 1989 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804-0807 
Dear Ms. Ellison: 
Thank you for your recent advice regarding my apparently somewhat 
unique Proposition 73 predicament. 
I have reviewed the materials you provided and do now agree 
that, given California Common Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles Superior 
Court No. C709383), the election campaign money I accumulated 
while a judge of the Municipal Court may not continue to be 
held or later spent to promote my election as judge of the 
Superior Court. 
From among the available courses we discussed, 
I have decided to use the funds to partially repay the still 
outstanding loans made in 1978 to my Municipal Court election 
committee. 
This will exhaust all funds on deposit leaving 
nothing to be carried over for a future, Superior Court election 
campaign even if a change in law permitting same should occur. 
We did not discuss the procedural requirements of such a transfer 
and disbursement, but it seems to me that the following described 
sequence is appropriate: 
1. 
Open a bank checking account in the name of my existing 
Municipal Court judge election controlled committee for the 
sole purpose of receiving funds from the Superior Court re-election 
FPPC Form 502 Campaign Bank Account. 
As I understand the existing 
regulations no new FPPC Form 502 relating to the establishment 
of this conduit account need be submitted. 
2. 
Transfer all money on deposit in the above Form 502 
account to the Municipal Court election account and thereby 
terminate the Form 502 account. 
Upon such transfer and deposit, 
file an amended FPPC Form 502 formally notifying the FPPC of 
such termination. 


Ms. Margaret Ellison 
March 14, 1989 
Page Two 
3. 
Upon closure of the FPPC Form 502 bank account, file 
with the Secretary of State a Form 415 Statement of Termination 
ending the existence of the Superior Court re-election committee 
which was formed for the sole purpose of controlling the FPCC 
Form 502 Campaign Bank Account. 
Also, file a new FPPC Form 
501 terminating my earlier announced intention to seek the 
office of Superior Court Judge in November 1994. 
4. 
From the Municipal Court election committee checking 
account repay campaign loans until the funds are exhausted 
and then, 
5. 
Terminate the Municipal Court election committee and 
close the controlled account. 
As I mentioned, I am anxious to comply with existing law as 
presently interpreted, exhaust all funds on hand, engage in 
no solicitation of campaign funds in the near future, and terminate 
all election committees. 
If you conclude any of my intended steps to these ends are 
inappropriate or not permitted, please so inform me. 
Sincerely, 
FREDERICK A. JONES 
Judge of the Superior Court 
FAJ:spg 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
March 22, 1989 
Honorable Frederick A. Jones 
Judge of the Superior Court 
Hall of Justice 
800 South Victoria Avenue 
Ventura, CA 
93009 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-169 
Dear Judge Jones: 
We received your letter requesting confirmation of advice 
under the Political Reform Act on March 20, 1989. 
Your letter has 
been assigned to Margaret Ellison for response. 
If you have any 
questions, you may contact Ms. Ellison directly at (916) 322-5901. 
If the letter is appropriate for confirmation without further 
analysis, we will attempt to expedite our response. 
A confirming 
response will be released after it has gone through our approval 
process. 
If ·the letter is not appropriate for this treatment, the 
staff person assigned to prepare the response will contact you 
shortly to advise you. 
In such cases, the normal analysis, review 
and approval process will be followed. 
You should be aware that your letter and our response are 
public records which may be disclosed to any interested person 
upon receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter. 
Very truly yours

Thank you for your cooperation in this matter. 
Very truly yours, 
FREDERICK A. JONES 
Judge of the Superior Court

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING COVER PAGE 
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION 
FORM 410 
NAME AND ADDRESS OF COMMITTEE: 
Provide the full name of the committee. 
A committee may use only one name. 
If this committee is 
controlled by an individual, entity or other person, the name of the individual, entity or other person must be 
included in the name of the committee. For example, when a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state 
ballot measure controls a committee, his or her name must be included in the name of the committee. 
There are special rules for "sponsored committees," see Part V 
In the case of a committee supporting or opposing a ballot measure as ItS principal activity, all 
references to the committee required by the Political Reform Act must include the statement, 
"Committee For Proposition 
," if supporting a measure, or "Committee Against Proposition 
," if opposing a measure. 
----
The street address of the committee must be reported 
A post office box number is NOT acceptable. 
The mailing address of the committee must be reported if it is different from the committee's street 
address. A post office box number IS acceptable for the mailing address. 
DATE QUALIFIED AS A COMMITTEE: 
Date qualified is the date the committee received contributions totaling $1,000 or more during a 
calendar year. 
If a Form 410 is filed prior to receipt of $1,000 in contributions, enter "Not Yet 
Qualified" in the "Date Qualified as Committee" space. 
I. 
TREASURER AND OTHER PRINCIPAL OFFICERS: 
The treasurer's name, home or business address, a mailing address, if different than home or business 
address, and a phone number where the treasurer can be reached during business hours must be 
listed. 
If the committee has "principal officers" other than the treasurer, such as a president, secretary or 
chairperson, they must be identified. 
II. IS THIS A BROAD BASED POLITICAL COMMITTEE? 
To qualify as a "broad based committee, H the committee may not be controlled by a candidate or 
officeholder, the committee must have been in existence for more than six months, must have received 
contributions from 100 or more persons at any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year (July 1 
- June 30) and the previous two fiscal years, and must have made contributions to five or more candidates at 
any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year and the previous two fiscal years. 
Enter the date on or before which the committee qualified as a broad based committee. If the committee 
qualified as a broad based committee on or before January 1, 1989, enter "On or before January 1, 1989. N 
III. IS THIS COMMITTEE CONTROLLED BY A CANDIDA TE, OFFICEHOLDER OR PROPONENT 
OF A STATE BALLOT MEASURE? 
A committee is controlled by a candidate, officehOlder or proponent of a state ballot measure if the 
candidate, officeholder or proponent, their agent or any other committee they (ontro/, has a significant 
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee. 
Be<ause of new provisions contained in Proposition 73 which was passed by the voters on June 7, 1988, 
candidates and officeholders may not jointly control committees formed to support their own candidacies, or 
committees formed to support or oppose other candidates. Candidates and officeholders may jointly control 
a committee whose only activity is supporting or opposing a ballot measure.

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING PAGE TWO 
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION 
FORM 410 
V 
SPONSORED COMMITTEE: 
"Sponsored committee" means a recipient committee, other than a candidate's or officeholder's controlled committee, 
which has one or more sponsors. 
Most political action committees are "sponsored committees" 
An organization, business or other entity is a sponsor if any of the following apply: 
(1) The committee receives all or nearly all of its contributions from the entity or organization, or its members, officers, 
employees, or shareholders; or 
(2) The entity or organization collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll deductions or dues from its 
members, officers or employees; or 
(3) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organization!: or entities, provides ail or nearly all of 
the administrative services for the committee; or 
(4) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations, sets the policies for soliciting 
contributions or making expenditures of committee funds. 
A sponsored committee (this includes most political action committees). must include the name of its sponsor in the name of 
the committee. If the sponsored committee has more than one sponsor and the sponsors are members of an industry or other 
identifiable group, the name of the committee must include a term identifying that industry or group. 
VI 
IF PRIMARIL Y FORMED TO SUPPORT OR OPPOSE SPECIFIC CANDIDA TES OR MEASURES, LIST 
SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR MEASURES SUPPORTED OR OPPOSED: 
A committee is "primarily formed" when its principal activity is raising or spending money to support or oppose a specific 
candidate or measure or a group of specific candidates all being voted upon in the same jurisdiction on the same date. This 
type of committee is usually formed for a specific election and then terminates after the election when all debts have been 
paid and there are no surplus funds. 
VII 
COMMITTEE'S PRINCIPAL ACTIVITY IF NOT SUPPORTING OR OPPOSING SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR 
MEASURES: 
A committee which does not support or oppose specific candidates or measures as its primary activity must provide a brief 
description of its political activities such as whether or not it supports candidates or measures which share a common political 
affiliation. 
VI/J 
YOU MUST SPECIFY WHA T DISPOSITION IvV/LL BE MADE OF SURPLUS FUNDS IN THE EVENT OF 
TERMINA TlON 
A committee must specify what use will be made of any surplus funds. Elections Code Section 12400, et seq. prohibits 
personal use of campaign funds and specifies permissible uses of surplus campaign funds. Elections Code Section 29795 
specifies permissible uses of funds received by state ballot measure committees. The Fair Political Practices Commission has no 
authority to interpret the Elections Code. Contact the enforcement official for your jurisdiction if you have any questions. 
VERI FICA TlON: 
The statement must be signed and verified by the committee treasurer. If this committee is controlled by a candidate, 
officeholder or state ballot Intla5Ui~ proponent, the candidate, officeholder or proponent must also sign and verity the 
statement. If a ballot measure committee is controlled by two or three candidates or officeholders, each candidate must sign 
the Statement of Organization. If more than three candidates or officeholders control a ballot measure committee, one of 
the candidates or officeholders may sign the Statement of Organization on behalf of all controlling candidates or 
officeholders.

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING COVER PAGE 
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION 
FORM 410 
NAME AND ADDRESS OF COMMITTEE: 
Provide the full name of the committee. 
A committee may use only one name. 
If this committee is 
controlled by an Individual, entity or other person, the name of the individual, entity or other person must be 
included in the name of the committee. For example, when a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state 
ballot measure controls a committee, his or her name must be included in the name of the committee. 
There are speCial rules for "sponsored committees," see Part v. 
In the case of a committee supporting or opposing a ballot measure as its principal activity, all 
references to the committee required by the Political Reform Act must include the statement, 
"Committee For Proposition 
" if supporting a measure, or "Committee Against Proposition 
," if opposing a measure. 
----
The street address of the committee must be reported. 
A post office box number is NOT acceptable. 
The mailing address of the committee must be reported if it is different from the committee's street 
address. A post office box number IS acceptable for the mailing address. 
DA TE QUALIFIED AS A COMMITTEE: 
Date qualified is the date the committee received contributions totaling $1,000 or more during a 
calendar year. 
If a Form 410 is filed prior to receipt of $1,000 in contributions, enter "Not Yet 
Qualified" in the "Date Qualified as Committee" space. 
I. 
TREASURER AND OTHER PRINCIPAL OFFICERS: 
The treasurer's name, home or business address, a mailing address, if different than home or business 
address, and a phone number where the treasurer can be reached during business hours must be 
listed. 
If the committee has "principal officers" other than the treasurer, such as a president, secretary or 
chairperson, they must be identified. 
II. IS THIS A BROAD BASED POLITICAL COMMITTEE? 
To qualify as a "broad based committee, H the committee may not be controlled by a candidate or 
officeholder, the committee must have been in existence for more than six months, must have received 
contributions from 100 or more persons at any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year (July 1 
- June 30) and the previous two fiscal years, and must have made contributions to five or more candidates at 
any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year and the previous two fiscal years. 
Enter the date on or before which the committee qualified as a broad based committee. If the committee 
qualified as a broad based committee on or before January 1, 1989, enter "On or before January 1, 1989. H 
III. IS THIS COMMITTEE CONTROLLED BY A CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR PROPONENT 
OF A STA TE BALLOT MEASURE? 
A committee is controlled by a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state ballot measure if the 
candidate, officeholder or proponent, the r agent or any other committee they control, has a significant 
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee. 
Because of new provisions contained in Proposition 73 which was passed by the voters on June 7, 1988, 
candidates and officeholders may not jointly control committees formed to support their own candidacies, or 
committees formed to support or oppose other candidates. Candidates and officeholders may jointly control 
a committee whose only activity is supporting or opposing a ballot measure.

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING PAGE TWO 
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION 
FORM 410 
V 
SPONSORED COMMITTEE: 
"Sponsored committee" means a recipient committee, other than a candidate's or officeholder's controlled committee, 
which has one or more sponsors. 
Most political action committees are "sponsored committees." 
An organization, business or other entity is a sponsor If any of the following apply: 
(1) The committee receives all or nearly all of its contributions from the entity or organization, or its members, officers, 
employees, or shareholders; or 
(2) The entity or organization collects contributions for the committee by use of payroll deductions or dues from its 
members, officers or employees; or 
(3) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations or entities, provides all or nearly all of 
the administrative services for the committee; or 
(4) The entity or organization, alone or in combination with other organizations, sets the policies for soliciting 
contributions or making expenditures of committee funds. 
A sponsored committee (this includes most political action committees), must include the name of its sponsor in the name of 
the committee. If the sponsored committee has more than one sponsor and the sponsors are members of an industry or other 
identifiable group, the name of the committee must include a term identifying that industry or group. 
VI 
IF PRIMARIL Y FORMED TO SUPPORT OR OPPOSE SPECIFIC CANDIDA TES OR MEASURES, LIST 
SPECIFIC CANDIDATES OR MEASURES SUPPORTED OR OPPOSED: 
A committee is "primarily formed" when its principal activity is raising or spending money to support or oppose a specific 
candidate or measure or a group of specific candidates all being voted upon in the same jurisdiction on the same date. This 
type of committee is usually formed for a specific election and then terminates after the election when all debts have been 
paid and there are no surplus funds. 
VII 
COMMITTEE'S PRINCIPAL ACTIVITY IF NOT SUPPORTING OR OPPOSING SPECIFIC CANDIDA TES OR 
MEASURES: 
A committee which does not support or oppose specific candidates or measures as its primary activity must provide a brief 
description of its political activities such as whether or not it supports candidates or measures which share a common political 
affiliation. 
VIII 
YOU MUST SPECIFY WHA T DISPOSITION WILL BE MADE OF SURPLUS FUNDS IN THE EVENT OF 
TERMINA nON 
A committee must specify what use will be made of any surplus funds. Elections Code Section 12400, et seq. prohibits 
personal use of campaign funds and specifies permissible uses of surplus campaign funds. Elections Code Section 29795 
specifies permissible uses of funds received by state ballot measure committees. The Fair Political Practices Commission has no 
authority to interpret the Elections Code. Contact the enforcement official for your jurisdiction if you have any questions. 
VERIFICATION: 
The statement must be signed and verified by the committee treasurer. If this committee is controlled by a candidate, 
officeholder or state ballot measure proponent, the candidate, officeholder o· proponent must also sign and verify the 
statement. If a ballot measure committee is controlled by two or three candidates or officeholders, each candidate must sign 
the Statement of Organization. If more than three candidates or officeholders control a ballot measure committee, one of 
the candidates or officeholders may sign the Statement of Organization on behalf of all controlling candidates Or 
officehol ders.

INSTRUCTIONS FOR PREPARING COVER PAGE 
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION 
FORM 410 
NAME AND ADDRESS OF COMMITTEE: 
Provide the full name of the committee. 
A committee may use only one name. 
If this committee is 
controlled by an individual, entity or other person, the name of the individual, entity or other person must be 
included in the name of the committee. For example, when a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state 
ballot measure controls a committee, his or her name must be included in the name of the committee. 
There are special rules for "sponsored committees," see Part V 
In the case of a committee supporting or opposing a ballot measure as ItS principal activity, all 
references to the committee required by the Political Reform Act must include the statement, 
"Committee For Proposition 
," if supporting a measure, or "Committee Against Proposition 
," if opposing a measure. 
----
The street address of the committee must be reported. 
A post office box number is NOT acceptable. 
The mailing address of the committee must be reported if it IS different from the committee's street 
address. A post office box number IS acceptable for the mailing address. 
DA TE QUALIFIED AS A COMMITTEE: 
Date qualified is the date the committee received contributions totaling $1,000 or more during a 
calendar year. 
If a Form 410 is filed prior to receipt of $1,000 in contributions, enter "Not Yet 
Qualified" in the "Date Qualified as Committee" space. 
I. 
TREASURER AND OTHER PRINCIPAL OFFICERS: 
The treasurer's name, home or business address, a mailing address, if different than home or business 
address, and a phone number where the treasurer can be reached during business hours must be 
listed. 
If the committee has "principal officers" other than the treasurer, such as a president, secretary or 
chairperson, they must be identified. 
II. IS THIS A BROAD BASED POLITICAL COMMITTEE? 
To qualify as a "broad based committee, H the committee may not be controlled by a candidate or 
officeholder, the committee must have been in existence for more than six months, must have received 
contributions from 100 or more persons at any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year (July 1 
- June 30) and the previous two fiscal years, and must have made contributions to five or more candidates at 
any time during the period covered by the current fiscal year and the previous two fiscal years. 
Enter the date on or before which the committee qualified as a broad based committee. If the committee 
qualified as a broad based committee on or before January 1, 1989, enter" On or before January 1, 1989." 
III. IS THIS COMMITTEE CONTROLLED BY A CANDIDATE, OFFICEHOLDER OR PROPONENT 
OF A STA TE BALLOT MEASURE? 
A committee is controlled by a candidate, officeholder or proponent of a state ballot measure if the 
candidate, officeholder or proponent, their agent or any other committee they control, has a significant 
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee. 
Because of new provisions contained in Proposition 73 which was passed by the voters on June 7, 1988, 
candidates and officeholders may not jointly control committees formed to support their own candidacies, or 
committees formed to support or oppose other candidates. Candidates and officeholders may jointly control 
a committee whose only activity is supporting or opposing a ballot measure.

QUESTION: s, you may contact Ms. Ellison directly at (916) 322-5901. 
If the letter is appropriate for confirmation without further
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 14, 1989 Honorable Frederick A. Jones Judge of the Superior Court 800 South Victoria Avenue Ventura, CA 93009 Dear Judge Jones: Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-89-169 This will confirm our telephone conversation of February 22, 1989, in which we discussed the use of your accumulated campaign funds in light of the decision in California Common Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles County Superior Court Case No. C709383.) Since those funds were acquired prior to January 1, 1989, they would be restricted funds to be used for any lawful purpose other than to support or oppose a candidate. (Government Code Section 85306.) Thus, even though these funds were composed of contributions within statutory limits and deposited in a campaign bank account for a future campaign, the funds could not be used for future campaign expenditures unless the California Common Cause decision were reversed on appeal. I sent you a copy of Section 18536.2 of the California Code of Regulations which sets forth appropriate uses of restricted funds. As I explained to you, the California Common Cause case is still subject to appeal. We have been advising that no action be taken with respect to the disposition of restricted funds pending the appeal. If the funds now are placed in a restricted account, that election probably could not be reversed in the event that the California Common Cause decision were reversed on appeal. Notwithstanding this advice, you have decided not to await the outcome of the California Common Cause decision. You would prefer to close your campaign bank account and use the accumulated funds to repay campaign loans owed by your 1978 Municipal Court election committee. You have outlined the steps that you believe would be appropriate to accomplish 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Boy R07 • ~"' ........ ~"" ...... ~ r" A n~ 0"" " .... ".... - I~ 4 - ~ - - - -- Honorable Frederick A. Jones April 14, 1989 Page 2 this result in your letter of March 14, 1989 (copy enclosed.) We have reviewed the proposed sequence and agree that it would be an acceptable way to proceed. If you have any questions about this letter, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901. DMG:MWE:aa Enclosure Sincerely, Diane M. Griffiths General Counsel '-7/!~$ By: Margaret W. Ellison Counsel, Legal Division Ms. Margaret Ellison Staff Attorney CHAMBERS OF 'lrur ~uprrior (tCourt FREDERICK A. JONES HALL OF JUSTICE 800 SOUTH VICTORIA AVENUE VENTURA, CA 93009 (805) 654-2915 March 14, 1989 Fair Political Practices Commission Post Office Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804-0807 Dear Ms. Ellison: Thank you for your recent advice regarding my apparently somewhat unique Proposition 73 predicament. I have reviewed the materials you provided and do now agree that, given California Common Cause v. FPPC (Los Angeles Superior Court No. C709383), the election campaign money I accumulated while a judge of the Municipal Court may not continue to be held or later spent to promote my
